title: Dry, frizzy, rough hair
excerpt: Dry, frizzy hair is often a sign that your body is running on too much Vata, the airy, mobile force that governs dryness throughout the body. Ayurveda offers a whole-body approach to restoring lustre, from the oils you put on your scalp to the food you eat at dinner.
body_html:
why your hair feels like straw

You wash your hair, let it dry, and somehow it still looks dull and rough by afternoon. It knots easily, breaks at the tips, and no amount of conditioner seems to help for long. If this sounds familiar, Ayurveda has a name for this condition: Ruksha Kesha, literally 'dry hair', understood as a local expression of excess Vata dosha in the body.

Vata is the force of air and space. When it rises, it pulls moisture out of tissues. The scalp becomes dry, the hair shaft loses its natural oils, and the result is the frizz and roughness that so many people struggle with. Seasonal shifts, irregular eating, too much stress, travelling frequently, and over-washing with harsh shampoos all drive Vata higher (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana). The classical texts note that the hair, nails, and joints are all seats where aggravated Vata first makes itself visible.

the hair, nails, and joints are all seats where aggravated Vata first makes itself visible

what the granthas say about nourishing hair

The Ashtanga Hridaya devotes considerable attention to Dinacharya, or daily self-care routines. Among these, Shiro Abhyanga, the practice of massaging warm oil into the scalp, is described as one of the most powerful ways to pacify Vata and bring nourishment to the head region (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana, Dinacharya chapter). Regular oil massage is said to strengthen the hair roots, improve scalp circulation, and promote sound sleep, because the head is considered the seat of Prana, the vital life force.

The Bhavaprakasha, a later but widely relied-upon text, discusses the properties of several oils and herbs useful for the hair and scalp. It emphasises that oils that are heavy, unctuous, and warming in quality counteract Vata most effectively (Bhavaprakasha, Haritakyadi Varga). Sesame oil in particular is described as the foremost oil for pacifying Vata and for feeding the hair. Coconut oil, with its cooling and lubricating properties, is also widely recommended in the southern Ayurvedic tradition for its ability to reduce scalp inflammation and restore moisture to the hair shaft.

Diet plays an equally important role. The classical framework is clear: a diet that is dry, raw, cold, or skipped entirely will aggravate Vata throughout the body, including the scalp (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana). Warm cooked foods, healthy fats like ghee, sesame seeds, soaked nuts, and root vegetables all help to ground Vata and deliver nourishment to the deeper tissues, including the hair follicles.

gentle habits that actually make a difference

Start with a warm oil massage one to two times a week. Warm a small amount of sesame or coconut oil in your palms and work it into the scalp in slow circular motions. Leave it on for at least thirty minutes before washing, or apply it the night before and wash in the morning. This single habit, done consistently, tends to produce visible change within four to six weeks.

Reduce how often you wash your hair. Daily shampooing strips the scalp of its natural oils and creates a cycle where the scalp produces more oil to compensate, but the hair shaft itself stays dry. Two to three washes a week with a mild, sulphate-free shampoo is usually enough for most hair types. After washing, avoid rubbing the hair with a towel. Gently squeeze out the water and let it air dry where possible, since heat from dryers pushes Vata higher.

A simple herbal rinse can make a real difference. Steep a handful of dried hibiscus petals or a tablespoon of amla powder in warm water for twenty minutes, strain it, and pour it over the hair after shampooing as a final rinse. Both hibiscus and amla are classical hair tonics described in Bhavaprakasha for their ability to restore lustre and reduce breakage.

On the food side, try to include a small portion of ghee in your daily meals, a handful of soaked almonds in the morning, and warm sesame-based dishes like til ladoo or til chutney a few times a week. These are not exotic prescriptions; they are ordinary Indian kitchen foods that happen to align very well with what Ayurveda recommends for Vata pacification. Drink enough warm water through the day, because dehydration shows up in the hair long before it shows up as thirst.

when to see a vaidya or doctor

Dry and frizzy hair is usually a manageable condition that responds well to lifestyle changes. However, there are situations where it points to something that needs professional attention. See a doctor if you notice sudden or patchy hair loss along with dryness, if your scalp has persistent itching, flaking, or red inflamed patches that do not settle, or if your hair is breaking and falling at a rate that worries you. These can be signs of conditions like thyroid imbalance, iron deficiency, fungal infections, or autoimmune skin conditions, all of which require proper diagnosis and treatment.

This article is general information intended to share the Ayurvedic perspective on dry hair. It is not a diagnosis, and it is not a substitute for medical care or the advice of a qualified vaidya who has examined you directly.

Try This Today

  • Weekly warm oil massage: Warm a small amount of sesame or coconut oil in your palms. Work it into the scalp in slow circular motions and leave it on for at least thirty minutes before washing. Do this once or twice a week.
  • Amla or hibiscus rinse: Steep one tablespoon of amla powder or a handful of dried hibiscus petals in warm water for twenty minutes. Strain and pour over the hair after shampooing as a final rinse.
  • Reduce wash frequency: Limit shampooing to two or three times a week using a mild, sulphate-free shampoo. Let the hair air dry and avoid rubbing with a towel.
  • Add ghee and sesame to meals: Include a small spoon of ghee in daily meals and eat sesame-based foods like til chutney or soaked almonds a few times a week to nourish hair from within.
author: Kerala Ayurveda Editorial Team

metafields.dek: Dry, frizzy hair is often a sign that your body is running on too much Vata, the airy, mobile force that governs dryness throughout the body. Ayurveda offers a whole-body approach to restoring lustre, from the oils you put on your scalp to the food you eat at dinner.
metafields.disclaimer: Ayurveda guidance on AyurHQ is supportive care, not emergency or diagnostic medical treatment.
at_a_glance: Dry, frizzy hair is a sign of excess Vata dosha drawing moisture out of the scalp and hair shaft.
Warm oil massage with sesame or coconut oil, done once or twice a week, is the most effective classical remedy.
A diet rich in ghee, sesame, soaked nuts, and warm cooked food nourishes hair from the inside out.

glossary_terms.definition: Dry hair; literally 'rough/dry' (ruksha) 'hair' (kesha), used in classical texts to describe hair that has lost its natural moisture and lustre.
One of the three doshas (biological forces); governed by air and space, it controls movement and dryness in the body.
Head oil massage; a classical self-care practice described in Dinacharya routines for nourishing the scalp and pacifying Vata.
Daily routine; the set of morning and evening self-care practices recommended in Ayurveda to maintain balance.
Vital life force; in Ayurveda, the head is considered its primary seat, making scalp care especially important.

faqs.question: What is the meaning of "Ruksha Kesha" in Ayurveda?
faqs.answer: In the Ayurvedic tradition, Ruksha Kesha is defined as: Dry hair; literally 'rough/dry' (ruksha) 'hair' (kesha), used in classical texts to describe hair that has lost its natural moisture and lustre.

faqs.question: How can I start addressing this issue today?
faqs.answer: One of the most recommended practices is to weekly warm oil massage. Warm a small amount of sesame or coconut oil in your palms. Work it into the scalp in slow circular motions and leave it on for at least thirty minutes before washing. Do this once or twice a week.

faqs.question: Are there any specific lifestyle habits I should adopt?
faqs.answer: A key suggestion is to amla or hibiscus rinse. Steep one tablespoon of amla powder or a handful of dried hibiscus petals in warm water for twenty minutes. Strain and pour over the hair after shampooing as a final rinse.
